data.result:
.env:1: # API keys are read from the environment only; never commit a real .env
.env:2: # Copy this file to .env and fill in your keys (.env is gitignored)
.env:3: #
.env:4: # copperhead loads .env from the directory you run it in. A variable already set
.env:5: # in your shell always wins over this file.
.env:6: OPENAI_API_KEY=
.env:7: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
.env:8: 
.env:9: # Optional: saved-login for the Claude Code provider (--model claude-code).
.env:10: # Drives Claude Code via the Claude Agent SDK and reuses your saved login, so a
.env:11: # Claude subscription runs copperhead with NO ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. Mint it with
.env:12: # `claude setup-token` while logged into Claude Code. The Claude Agent SDK
.env:13: # (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`) ships as an optional dependency, so a normal
.env:14: # install includes it. copperhead never reads or logs this credential; the CLI
.env:15: # owns authentication.
.env:16: CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN=
.env:17: 
.env:18: # Optional: default model. Overridden by --model, and it overrides the `model`
.env:19: # field in .copperhead/config.json.
.env:20: #
.env:21: #   claude-code       Claude Code, saved login (no API key; uses the token above)
.env:22: #   claude-code:<id>  the same, on a specific model id
.env:23: #   claude            the Anthropic API provider on its default model
.env:24: #   claude-<id>       any Anthropic API model id, passed through as-is
.env:25: #   codex             local Codex CLI using its saved ChatGPT login (no API key)
.env:26: #   codex:<id>        local Codex CLI with an explicit model id
.env:27: #   gpt-5             the OpenAI provider on its default model
.env:28: #   <anything>        sent to OpenAI as a model id, e.g. gpt-5-mini
.env:29: #
.env:30: # Routing is by prefix, matched top to bottom: "claude-code" is checked before
.env:31: # the "claude" prefix (Anthropic API, needs ANTHROPIC_API_KEY); "codex" uses the
.env:32: # local Codex CLI; everything else goes to OpenAI and needs OPENAI_API_KEY. A
.env:33: # typo like "claud-sonnet-5" routes to OpenAI and fails there.
.env:34: #
.env:35: # Left blank, copperhead picks a provider from whichever key is present,
.env:36: # preferring OPENAI_API_KEY if both are set.
.env:37: COPPERHEAD_MODEL=claude-code
.env:38: 
.env:39: # Optional: path to the authenticated local Codex CLI. The default is `codex`
.env:40: # on PATH. Only needed for non-standard installations.
.env:41: COPPERHEAD_CODEX_PATH=
.env:42: 
.env:43: # Optional: cross-run memory via Synap (https://docs.maximem.ai). Left blank,
.env:44: # copperhead runs exactly as before with no network calls to Synap.
.env:45: #
.env:46: # Set it and each run recalls relevant context from your earlier runs (on this
.env:47: # board and others) into the system prompt, then records its outcome, decisions,
.env:48: # and refusals back. In-repo docs and the KiCad files stay the source of truth;
.env:49: # recalled memory is advisory context layered on top.
.env:50: #
.env:51: # Requires the optional @maximem/synap-js-sdk package AND a Python 3.11+ runtime
.env:52: # on the host: the JS SDK drives a Python bridge as a subprocess. If either is
.env:53: # missing, copperhead logs a line and continues without memory.
.env:54: SYNAP_API_KEY=
.env:55: 
.env:56: # Optional memory scoping. SYNAP_USER_ID defaults to your `git config
.env:57: # user.email`; SYNAP_CUSTOMER_ID defaults to "copperhead" and only matters on
.env:58: # B2B Synap instances.
.env:59: SYNAP_USER_ID=
.env:60: SYNAP_CUSTOMER_ID=
.env:61: 
brief.md:1: # Brief: USB-C power breakout
brief.md:2: 
brief.md:3: ## What it is
brief.md:4: 
brief.md:5: A small breakout board that takes USB-C 5V from a normal charger and presents it on screw terminals and a 0.1" header, for powering breadboard projects. No data lines, no negotiation beyond default 5V.
brief.md:6: 
brief.md:7: ## Must do
brief.md:8: 
brief.md:9: 1. Accept a USB-C receptacle, sink side, 5V only.
brief.md:10: 2. Present 5V and GND on a 2-pin 3.5mm screw terminal and on a 2x2 0.1" header.
brief.md:11: 3. Present itself as a plain 5V sink so a USB-C source turns on VBUS.
brief.md:12: 4. Show a power LED.
brief.md:13: 5. Protect against a short on the output.
brief.md:14: 
brief.md:15: ## Budgets
brief.md:16: 
brief.md:17: - Output current: the board must be rated for 3A continuous.
brief.md:18: - Input voltage: 5V nominal, survive 6V.
brief.md:19: - Quiescent current with no load: under 2mA including the LED.
brief.md:20: - Board area: 30mm x 20mm or smaller.
brief.md:21: 
brief.md:22: ## Constraints
brief.md:23: 
brief.md:24: - 2-layer, 1oz copper, standard JLCPCB process, no controlled impedance.
brief.md:25: - Hand-solderable: nothing finer than 0603, no BGA, no QFN with a thermal pad.
brief.md:26: - Target BOM cost under $3 at qty 100.
brief.md:27: - Through-hole mounting holes: 2x M3, on a 24mm pitch.
brief.md:28: 
brief.md:29: ## Out of scope
brief.md:30: 
brief.md:31: - USB-PD or any voltage other than 5V.
brief.md:32: - Data pass-through.
brief.md:33: - Enclosure.
brief.md:34: 
brief.md:35: ## Notes
brief.md:36: 
brief.md:37: The CC pull-down resistors are what make this work at all: without an Rd on each CC line, a compliant USB-C source never turns on VBUS. State the value chosen and why in `docs/DECISIONS.md`, since it is the single thing most likely to be wrong.
brief.md:38: 
brief.md:39: Note that a sink does not decide how much current it gets. The source advertises its capability with an Rp on CC, and the sink is expected to read that and stay within it. This board has no MCU to read it, so "3A" here means the copper, connector, and protection are rated for 3A, not that 3A is always available. Say so in `docs/SPEC.md` rather than implying the board negotiates anything.
brief.md:40:

data.text:
# Plan

**What will change:** Create `docs/SPEC.md` seeding the requirements for the USB-C power breakout (device description, top-level constraints, and every budget from the brief, each recorded as a constraint). Since no `openspec/` workspace exists in this repo yet, I will not seed `openspec/specs/` (the conditional in the task doesn't apply) — I'll note that explicitly rather than fabricate a workspace.

**Files affected:** `docs/SPEC.md` (new).

**Constraints/budgets at risk of ambiguity (will flag ASSUMED where the brief is silent):**
- Operating temperature range — not stated.
- Short-circuit protection trip threshold/method — brief says "protect against a short," doesn't specify fuse type or trip current.
- LED current budget — must fit inside the 2 mA quiescent budget; brief doesn't allocate how much of that 2 mA the LED gets.
- ESD/surge protection on the USB-C connector — not stated.
- CC line Rd value itself is explicitly deferred to `docs/DECISIONS.md` per the brief's note (not a spec-stage task) — SPEC.md will state the *requirement* (present Rd on each CC pin per USB-C spec so VBUS enables) without fixing the resistor value.

I'll record every budget (stated and assumed) with `record_constraint` once available, and log decisions/assumptions with `record_decision` as I go.

args.why: Seed docs/SPEC.md from the product brief so downstream pipeline stages (schematic, layout) have an authoritative source for the device description, budgets, and constraints before any part selection begins.
args.what_changes:
- Add docs/SPEC.md: device description, top-level constraints, and every budget from the brief (output current rating vs. negotiated availability, input voltage range, quiescent current, board area, layer/process constraints, hand-solderable rule, BOM cost target, mounting hole spec).
- Flag brief-silent items as ASSUMED with sensible defaults: operating temperature range, short-circuit protection method/trip point, LED current allocation within the quiescent budget, ESD/surge protection on the USB-C connector.
- Explicitly state that this board does not negotiate USB-PD current — 3A is a copper/connector/protection rating, not a guaranteed available current, per brief's note.
- Defer the CC pull-down (Rd) *value* and rationale to docs/DECISIONS.md (future stage); SPEC.md states only the functional requirement that each CC pin needs an Rd to GND so a compliant source enables VBUS.
- No openspec/ workspace exists in this repo, so openspec/specs/ is not seeded this run.
args.tasks:

- [ ] Mark ASSUMED defaults inline for: operating temperature, short-circuit protection trip current/method, LED current budget slice, ESD/surge protection.
- [ ] Record every budget (stated + assumed) with record_constraint.
- [ ] Record key assumptions/decisions with record_decision.
- [ ] Validate change, then write the file.

args.content:
# SPEC: USB-C Power Breakout

## 1. Overview

A small passive breakout board that accepts USB-C 5V (sink side, default USB
current only — no PD, no BC1.2, no negotiation) from a standard charger or
host port, and presents that 5V/GND on a 2-pin 3.5mm screw terminal and a
2x2 0.1" header for powering breadboard projects. There are no data lines,
no microcontroller, and no active negotiation logic on this board.

The board's only job is to (a) look like a legal USB-C 5V sink so a
compliant source turns on VBUS, and (b) get that 5V to screw terminals and a
header without catching fire if the output is shorted.

## 2. Functional requirements

1. Accept power from a USB-C receptacle, wired as sink-side only, 5V rail.
2. Present VBUS (5V) and GND on:
   - a 2-pin, 3.5mm-pitch screw terminal, and
   - a 2x2, 0.1" (2.54mm) pin header.
3. Present itself electrically as a plain 5V sink: each CC pin (CC1, CC2)
   terminates through a pull-down resistor (Rd) to GND, per the USB Type-C
   spec, so that a compliant USB-C source detects a sink attach and enables
   VBUS. No CC logic, no e-marker, no PD PHY. The Rd resistor **value** and
   the reasoning behind it are recorded in `docs/DECISIONS.md` (this is
   flagged there as the single detail most likely to be wrong).
4. Provide a visible power-good LED, active whenever VBUS is present.
5. Protect the output against a downstream short circuit without damaging
   the board, the connector, or the upstream source.

## 3. Budgets and constraints (authoritative — every numeric budget below is
also recorded as a machine-readable constraint)

| Budget | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Output current rating | 3A continuous | This is a **rating of the copper, connector, and protection device**, not a negotiated guarantee. See §3.1. |
| Input voltage, nominal | 5V | From USB-C VBUS, default (unnegotiated) level. |
| Input voltage, survive | up to 6V | Board must not be damaged by transients/out-of-spec sources up to 6V; not a normal operating point. |
| Quiescent current, no load | < 2 mA | Includes the power LED. See §3.2 for the LED's slice of this budget. |
| Board area | ≤ 30mm x 20mm | Outline constraint for layout stage. |
| BOM cost | < $3 at qty 100 | Target for part selection stage. |
| Mounting holes | 2x M3, through-hole, 24mm pitch | Mechanical constraint for layout stage. |

### 3.1 On the meaning of "3A"

A USB-C sink does not decide how much current it draws — the **source**
advertises what it can supply via the pull-up (Rp) it places on CC, and a
compliant sink is expected to read that advertisement and stay within it.
This board has no MCU and reads nothing on CC beyond presenting Rd; it
cannot detect or enforce a current limit set by the source. Therefore "the
board is rated for 3A" means: the trace widths/copper weight, the USB-C
receptacle's power pins, the screw terminal, the header, and the
short-circuit protection device are all specified to carry/withstand 3A
continuously. It does **not** mean the board negotiates, requests, or is
guaranteed 3A from any given source — a source advertising only 500mA/900mA
via its Rp will simply not supply more than that, and this board has no way
to ask for more. This must not be implied anywhere downstream (schematic
notes, silkscreen, BOM) as PD or negotiated current capability.

### 3.2 Quiescent current allocation (ASSUMED)

The brief states a hard budget (< 2mA total, LED included) but does not
split it between the LED and any other quiescent draw (e.g. a protection
IC's own supply current, if the chosen short-circuit protection device is
active rather than purely passive/PTC). ASSUMED default allocation, to be
held during part selection:

- LED branch (LED forward current through its series resistor): ≤ 1 mA.
- Everything else quiescent (protection device bias current, etc.): ≤ 1 mA.
- Combined must stay under the 2 mA hard budget with margin.

This split is a starting allocation for part selection, not a brief
requirement; revisit if the chosen protection IC's datasheet Iq forces a
different split.

## 4. Manufacturing / process constraints

- 2-layer PCB, 1oz copper, standard JLCPCB process. No controlled
  impedance requirement (no high-speed data on this board).
- Hand-solderable only: no passive smaller than 0603, no BGA packages, no
  QFN with an exposed/soldered thermal pad (thermal-pad QFN is excluded
  even if hand-solderable in principle, because reflow/hot-air is assumed
  unavailable for this build).
- 2x M3 through-hole mounting holes on a 24mm pitch.

## 5. Environmental / other constraints not stated in the brief (ASSUMED)

The brief does not state these; sensible commercial-grade defaults are
assumed and flagged here so they're visible before part selection locks
them in:

- **Operating temperature range: -20°C to +70°C (ASSUMED).** Ordinary
  indoor/breadboard-bench use; not an automotive or outdoor spec. Revisit
  if the product brief ever mentions an operating environment.
- **Short-circuit protection method: resettable, auto-recovering current
  limiter (e.g. eFuse/PTC-style), trip threshold in the 3.5–4A range
  (ASSUMED).** Chosen so normal 3A continuous operation has headroom before
  tripping, while a dead short is cleared without destroying the board or
  requiring a fuse replacement. Exact part chosen and its trip
  current/quiescent draw will be recorded in `docs/DECISIONS.md` at the
  part-selection stage, and must be checked against the §3.2 quiescent
  budget.
- **ESD / surge protection on VBUS: a low-capacitance TVS diode across
  VBUS/GND at the USB-C connector (ASSUMED).** The USB-C receptacle is a
  user-facing, cable-mated connector and is a plausible ESD entry point;
  brief is silent on this but it's standard practice for an exposed
  connector. Revisit at part-selection if this conflicts with the $3 BOM
  target.

## 6. Out of scope (per brief)

- USB-PD, or any output voltage other than 5V.
- USB data pass-through (D+/D- are not routed to anything).
- An enclosure.

## 7. Traceability

Source: `brief.md` ("Brief: USB-C power breakout"). Every budget in §3 is
also recorded via `record_constraint` in `.copperhead/constraints.json`.
Items marked ASSUMED in §3.2 and §5 are defaults proposed at this stage,
not stated in the brief, and are open for revision at the part-selection /
schematic stages.

data.name: write_file

## 1. Overview

current only — no PD, no BC1.2, no negotiation) from a standard charger or

no microcontroller, and no active negotiation logic on this board.

compliant source turns on VBUS, and (b) get that 5V to screw terminals and a
header without catching fire if the output is shorted.

## 2. Functional requirements

   flagged there as the single detail most likely to be wrong).

### 3.2 Quiescent current allocation (ASSUMED)

The brief states a hard budget (< 2mA total, LED included) but does not

- LED branch (LED forward current through its series resistor): ≤ 1 mA.
- Everything else quiescent (protection device bias current, etc.): ≤ 1 mA.
- Combined must stay under the 2 mA hard budget with margin.

This split is a starting allocation for part selection, not a brief

## 4. Manufacturing / process constraints

- 2-layer PCB, 1oz copper, standard JLCPCB process. No controlled

The brief does not state these; sensible commercial-grade defaults are

data.text:
## Progress check

All constraint revisit obligations are now resolved (the batch call cleared them; the trailing individual calls were redundant leftovers and errored harmlessly since those items were already resolved). Now I'll record the key decisions from this stage.

args.decision: docs/SPEC.md written this run seeds all budgets from brief.md verbatim into a table (§3), plus an explicit §3.1 clarifying that the 3A output rating is a copper/connector/protection rating, not a negotiated current guarantee.
args.rationale: Brief explicitly warned this is the detail most likely to be misread as PD negotiation; stating it plainly in SPEC.md prevents that misinterpretation from propagating into schematic notes or silkscreen at later stages.

data.name: record_decision

args.decision: docs/SPEC.md written this run seeds all budgets from brief.md verbatim into a table (§3), plus an explicit §3.1 clarifying that the 3A output rating is a copper/connector/protection rating, not a negotiated current guarantee.
args.rationale: Brief explicitly warned this is the detail most likely to be misread as PD negotiation; stating it plainly in SPEC.md prevents that misinterpretation from propagating into schematic notes or silkscreen at later stages.

data.result: decision recorded

args.decision: Assumed operating temperature range -20°C to +70°C (not stated in brief.md).
args.rationale: Commercial/bench-use default for a breadboard power breakout; no automotive/outdoor/industrial cue in the brief. Flagged ASSUMED in docs/SPEC.md §5 for revisit if a real environment spec ever surfaces.
args.affects: docs/SPEC.md; future component temperature-rating checks at part-selection

args.decision: Assumed short-circuit protection method: resettable auto-recovering current limiter (eFuse/PTC-style) with trip threshold in 3.5-4A range, rather than a one-time fuse.
args.rationale: Brief requires short-circuit protection but doesn't specify method; auto-recovering protection avoids a dead board after a transient short (better fit for a breadboard prototyping tool), with trip point set above the 3A continuous rating for headroom. Exact part deferred to part-selection stage and will be logged in docs/DECISIONS.md then.
args.affects: docs/SPEC.md §5; protection device selection at part-selection stage

args.decision: Assumed a 2mA quiescent budget split of ≤1mA for the LED branch and ≤1mA for everything else (e.g. protection IC bias current), rather than the brief's undivided 2mA total.
args.rationale: The brief gives one combined ceiling; splitting it now gives part-selection a concrete target for the LED+resistor pair versus the protection device, with margin under the 2mA hard limit. Not a brief requirement — open to revision if the chosen protection IC's actual Iq forces a different split.
args.affects: docs/SPEC.md §3.2; LED/resistor and protection-IC selection at part-selection stage

args.decision: docs/SPEC.md states the functional requirement that each CC pin needs a pull-down (Rd) to GND to enable VBUS from a compliant source, but the Rd's specific value and rationale are deferred to docs/DECISIONS.md at the schematic/part-selection stage.
args.rationale: Brief explicitly directs that the Rd value and reasoning be recorded in docs/DECISIONS.md, not SPEC.md, since it's the detail most likely to be wrong; SPEC.md stays at the requirements level (a passive sink must present Rd) without fixing a resistor value ahead of the stage that actually assigns it.

args.decision: Assumed ESD/surge protection on VBUS: a low-capacitance TVS diode across VBUS/GND at the USB-C connector.
args.rationale: The USB-C receptacle is a user-facing, cable-mated connector and a plausible ESD entry point; brief is silent but this is standard practice. Flagged ASSUMED in docs/SPEC.md §5, to be revisited against the $3 BOM budget at part-selection.
args.affects: docs/SPEC.md §5; TVS selection and BOM cost check at part-selection stage

Exact part deferred to part-selection stage and will be logged in docs/DECISIONS.md then.

data.text:
## Summary

Stage 1 (spec-seed) complete.

**Created:** `docs/SPEC.md` — full requirements seed for the USB-C power breakout:
- §1–2: device description and functional requirements (USB-C 5V sink, screw terminal + 2x2 header output, CC pull-down requirement, power LED, short-circuit protection).
- §3: every budget from the brief in a table (3A output rating, 5V nominal/6V survive input, <2mA quiescent, 30x20mm board area, <$3 BOM, 2xM3@24mm mounting holes), each also recorded via `record_constraint`.
- §3.1: explicit statement that "3A" is a copper/connector/protection rating, not a negotiated guarantee — per the brief's specific warning.
- §3.2 & §5: ASSUMED defaults flagged for items the brief didn't state — operating temp (-20 to 70°C), short-circuit protection method (auto-recovering limiter, 3.5–4A trip), quiescent budget split (≤1mA LED / ≤1mA other), ESD TVS on VBUS.
- §4, §6, §7: manufacturing constraints, out-of-scope list, traceability back to brief.md.

**Deliberately deferred:** the CC pull-down (Rd) **value** and its rationale — brief explicitly directs that into `docs/DECISIONS.md` at a later (schematic) stage, not into SPEC.md.

**Not done:** seeding `openspec/specs/` — no `openspec/` workspace exists in this repo; recorded that as an explicit decision rather than fabricating one.

All 11 budgets/assumptions recorded as constraints, all revisit obligations resolved, all key decisions logged in `docs/DECISIONS.md`. No schematic/board exists yet, so ERC/DRC/drift checks don't apply at this stage.

data.name: finish
data.result: error: missing required string arg "outcome"

args.summary: Stage 1 (spec-seed) complete. Wrote docs/SPEC.md covering: device overview, functional requirements 1-5 from the brief, a budgets table (output current rating 3A, input voltage 5V nominal/6V survive, quiescent current <2mA including LED, board area ≤30x20mm, BOM cost <$3 @ qty100, mounting holes 2xM3 @24mm pitch), an explicit §3.1 stating the 3A rating is a copper/connector/protection spec and not a negotiated PD guarantee, manufacturing/process constraints (2-layer 1oz JLCPCB, hand-solderable limits), out-of-scope items, and an ASSUMED section (§5) for items the brief didn't state: operating temp -20..70°C, short-circuit protection method (auto-recovering limiter, 3.5-4A trip), quiescent-budget split between LED and other draw, and a TVS on VBUS for ESD. The CC pull-down (Rd) value itself was deliberately left out of SPEC.md per the brief's explicit instruction to record that value and its rationale in docs/DECISIONS.md at a later stage — SPEC.md only states the functional requirement that Rd must be present. All 11 stated/assumed budgets were recorded via record_constraint and all resulting revisit obligations were resolved. Six decisions were logged in docs/DECISIONS.md documenting each assumption and the choice to skip seeding openspec/specs/ (no openspec/ workspace exists in this repo). This is a docs-only stage with no schematic or board yet, so ERC/DRC/drift checks are not applicable and were correctly skipped.

data.name: finish

data.name: finish

args.summary: Stage 1 (spec-seed) complete. Wrote docs/SPEC.md covering: device overview, functional requirements 1-5 from the brief, a budgets table (output current rating 3A, input voltage 5V nominal/6V survive, quiescent current <2mA including LED, board area ≤30x20mm, BOM cost <$3 @ qty100, mounting holes 2xM3 @24mm pitch), an explicit §3.1 stating the 3A rating is a copper/connector/protection spec and not a negotiated PD guarantee, manufacturing/process constraints (2-layer 1oz JLCPCB, hand-solderable limits), out-of-scope items, and an ASSUMED section (§5) for items the brief didn't state: operating temp -20..70°C, short-circuit protection method (auto-recovering limiter, 3.5-4A trip), quiescent-budget split between LED and other draw, and a TVS on VBUS for ESD. The CC pull-down (Rd) value itself was deliberately left out of SPEC.md per the brief's explicit instruction to record that value and its rationale in docs/DECISIONS.md at a later stage — SPEC.md only states the functional requirement that Rd must be present. All 11 stated/assumed budgets were recorded via record_constraint and all resulting revisit obligations were resolved. Six decisions were logged in docs/DECISIONS.md documenting each assumption and the choice to skip seeding openspec/specs/ (no openspec/ workspace exists in this repo). check_drift ran and reports vacuously clean (no schematic exists yet). This is a docs-only stage with no schematic or board yet, so ERC/DRC are not applicable at this stage.

data.result: all gates satisfied; run will commit
